coredata.dc:title: Design and fabrication of dual functional sulfur nanodots with reducibility and fluorescence properties for sensitive and selective analysis of metal ions in environmental water samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hanqiang
Li, Yufei
Gan, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Reduced-state sulfur nanodots (r-SDs) with reducibility, satisfactory fluorescence properties and high stability were synthesized for the sensitive and selective analysis of metal ions, including Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+. The reduction property of SDs was discovered for the first time, and the detailed reduction mechanisms of r-SDs were investigated. The reducibility of r-SDs exhibits the in situ reduction of Au3+ to Au nanoparticles (AuNPs/r-SDs) and also the reduction of Fe3+ and Cu2+ to Fe2+ and Cu+, respectively. On the one hand, selective analysis of Hg2+ can be achieved through dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric probes of thymine-modified AuNPs/r-SDs (T-AuNPs/r-SDs) on account of the special bonding of T-Hg2+-T, as verified by density functional theory simulations. Furthermore, the fluorescent probes r-SDs-1, 10-Phenanthroline (r-SDs-Phen) and r-SDs-neocuproine (r-SDs-Nc) were applied to the quantitative analysis of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by the specific recognition of Phen-Fe2+ and Nc-Cu+ after the reduction of Fe3+ and Cu2+ to Fe2+ and Cu+ by r-SDs. The linear ranges of T-AuNPs/r-SDs, r-SDs-Phen, and r-SDs-Nc for Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ were 5.0–70.0 nmol/L (fluorescence), 10.0–500.0 nmol/L (colorimetric), 1.0–100.0 μmol/L, and 1.0–50.0 μmol/L, with limits of detection of 0.82 nmol/L, 4.05 nmol/L, 0.36 μmol/L, and 0.56 μmol/L, respectively. In addition, good recoveries for practical applications in environmental water samples can be obtained to confirm the analysis accuracy. The successful results provided a new idea for the application of nanomaterials with reducibility and fluorescence properties in analytical chemistry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reducibility
Fluorescence
Optical probes
Reduced-state sulfur nanodots
Metal ions

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ in environmental water samples
Development of an analytical method for Hg2+
Development of an analytical method for Fe3+ and Cu2+
Selectivity and stability of the developed probes
Applications in environmental water sample analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University for supporting this research. The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for materials characterizations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and HRTEM images of r-SDs. (B) Fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of r-SDs. (C) XPS full spectrum, (D) C 1 s spectra, (E) O 1 s spectra, and (F) S 2p spectra of r-SDs.

(A and B) UV–vis absorption spectra of synthetic AuNPs with various substances. UV–vis absorption spectra of the (D and E) Phen + Fe3+ system and (G and H) Nc + Cu2+ system with various substances. (C) S 2p spectra of AuNPs/r-SDs, (F) Fe 2p spectra of r-SDs + Fe3+, and (I) Cu 2p spectra of r-SDs + Cu2+.
Schematic diagram of the (A) reaction process and (B) energy change.

(A) Fluorescence and (B) UV–vis absorbance spectra of a sensor system in the presence of various Hg2+ concentrations. The calibration curve of (C) fluorescence and (D) UV–vis absorbance versus Hg2+ concentrations.

Fluorescence spectra of the (A) r-SDs + Phen system and (B) r-SDs + Nc system in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+. Linear equations of F
0
/F versus (C) Fe3+ and (D) Cu2+ concentrations.

Selectivity and salinity tolerance of the developed method for (A) Hg2+ (Hg2+: 50 nmol/L, other metal ions: 5 μmol/L), (B) Fe3+ (Fe3+: 50 μmol/L, other metal ions: 50 μmol/L) and (C) Cu2+ (Cu2+: 50 μmol/L, other metal ions: 50 μmol/L) over other interfering substances (Na+: 10 mmol/L).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of r-SDs (A) for the analysis of Hg2+ (B) and Fe3+ and Cu2+ (C).

head.ce:title: Design and fabrication of dual functional sulfur nanodots with reducibility and fluorescence properties for sensitive and selective analysis of metal ions in environmental water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou 510275, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University, Guangzhou 510275, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reduced-state sulfur nanodots (r-SDs) with reducibility, satisfactory fluorescence properties and high stability were synthesized for the sensitive and selective analysis of metal ions, including Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+. The reduction property of SDs was discovered for the first time, and the detailed reduction mechanisms of r-SDs were investigated. The reducibility of r-SDs exhibits the in situ reduction of Au3+ to Au nanoparticles (AuNPs/r-SDs) and also the reduction of Fe3+ and Cu2+ to Fe2+ and Cu+, respectively. On the one hand, selective analysis of Hg2+ can be achieved through dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric probes of thymine-modified AuNPs/r-SDs (T-AuNPs/r-SDs) on account of the special bonding of T-Hg2+-T, as verified by density functional theory simulations. Furthermore, the fluorescent probes r-SDs-1, 10-Phenanthroline (r-SDs-Phen) and r-SDs-neocuproine (r-SDs-Nc) were applied to the quantitative analysis of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by the specific recognition of Phen-Fe2+ and Nc-Cu+ after the reduction of Fe3+ and Cu2+ to Fe2+ and Cu+ by r-SDs. The linear ranges of T-AuNPs/r-SDs, r-SDs-Phen, and r-SDs-Nc for Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ were 5.0–70.0 nmol/L (fluorescence), 10.0–500.0 nmol/L (colorimetric), 1.0–100.0 μmol/L, and 1.0–50.0 μmol/L, with limits of detection of 0.82 nmol/L, 4.05 nmol/L, 0.36 μmol/L, and 0.56 μmol/L, respectively. In addition, good recoveries for practical applications in environmental water samples can be obtained to confirm the analysis accuracy. The successful results provided a new idea for the application of nanomaterials with reducibility and fluorescence properties in analytical chemistry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-free quantum dots (MF-QDs) including carbon QDs, [1] graphene QDs, [2] black phosphorus QDs, [3] carbon nitride QDs, [4] and silicon QDs [5] have attracted significant attention due to their promising water solubility, satisfactory luminescence properties, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility. Apart from the abovementioned traditional advantages, several studies have reported that MF-QDs still exhibit the unique characteristic of reducibility in the in situ reduction of Au3+ (or Ag+) to Au (or Ag) nanoparticles (AuNPs, AgNPs) without extra reducing agents [6–8]. Based on the thermal, optical, and chemical properties of metal nanoparticles, many applications, including catalysis, [8] antibacterial, [6] and optoelectronic devices, [7] have been reported based on metal nanoparticles synthesized by in situ reduction with MF-QDs. However, the application of reducibility still ignores the luminescence properties of MF-QDs, limiting its applications in analytical science. Thus, using the reducibility of MF-QDs to design and fabricate new sensors with luminescence properties that can be widely used in analytical science is of great importance.

Sulfur nanodots (SDs) are new zero-dimensional MF-QDs with several advantages, such as ease of preparation, promising luminescence properties, favorable biocompatibility, and good antibacterial effects [9,10]. The reported preparation methods for SDs are phase transfer, [11] solvothermal, [12] mechanochemical, [13] microwave, [14] ultrasonic, [15] and hydrothermal methods, [16] which provide more possibilities in the synthesis of r-SDs. In general, SDs are primarily composed of various sulfur ions, elemental sulfur (luminescent center), and traditional organic ligands, which is different from typical MF-QDs [10,17]. In addition, elemental sulfur at the nanoscale is usually dispersed in organic ligands, including polyethylene glycol, [12] β-cyclodextrin, [18] carboxymethyl cellulose, [19] and polyvinyl alcohol, [20] ensuring promising luminescence properties, good stability and dispersity of SDs. However, the practical applications of SDs are still limited to antimicrobials, [21,22] sensors, [23,24] and fluorescence imaging, [15,19] suggesting great potential in searching for more characteristics and broadening the scope of analytical applications. The various reductive functional groups including
S
O
3
2 -
, S2−, or
S
x
2 -
on the surface of SDs provide significant reducibility compared to previous MF-QDs [9,10,23]. Considering the promising luminescence properties and potential reducibility of SDs, the development of universal analytical methods by combining luminescence with reducibility is highly anticipated.

Recently, several public safety issues caused by heavy metal pollution have received great attention because of their wide distribution in the environment and high toxicity to human health. Among several heavy metal ions, mercury ions (Hg2+) cause permanent and irreversible damage to the heart, kidneys, and nervous system, as well as great risks to organisms and the environment due to their bioaccumulation ability [25–27]. The maximum allowable concentrations of Hg2+ are 30 and 10 nmol/L in drinking water with guidelines set by the World Health Organization (WHO) and U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), respectively [28,29]. In addition, iron ions (Fe3+) and copper ions (Cu2+) are trace essential elements in organisms that play important roles in basic physiological processes [30,31]. Several diseases including liver and kidney damage, heart failure, diabetes, methemoglobinemia, iron deficiency anemia (for Fe3+), Wilson disease, Alzheimer's disease, and Menkes disease (for Cu2+), can be caused by abnormal concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ [32,33]. Similarly, the maximum allowable concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in drinking water and food are 5.4 and 20 μmol/L, respectively, set by the EPA [30,34,35]. Hence, the development of a rapid, selective and sensitive method for the analysis of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ is still needed. Traditional atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma, and inductively coupled plasmamass spectrometry shows limitations in terms of time consumption, expensive instruments and complex procedures. However, the wide distribution and source of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ put forward higher requirements in the development of reliable methods for rapid, sensitive and selective monitoring of the concentrations of the above metal ions.

In this work, three optical probes were designed and fabricated for the sensitive and selective analysis of metal ions including Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ based on the promising reducibility, satisfactory fluorescence property and high stability of reduced-state sulfur nanodots (r-SDs). The detailed reduction mechanisms and fluorescence performance of r-SDs were investigated in detail. On the one hand, the r-SDs endow in situ reduction of Au3+ to AuNPs (AuNPs/r-SDs) thanks to the reducibility of r-SDs, followed by modification of thymine to form dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric probes of T-AuNPs/r-SDs for Hg2+ analysis based on the special bonding of T-Hg2+-T ( Scheme 1B). On the other hand, fluorescent probes of r-SDs-1,10-Phenanthroline (r-SDs-Phen) and r-SDs-neocuproine (r-SDs-Nc) were applied to quantitative analysis of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by specific recognition of Phen-Fe2+ and Nc-Cu+ due to the effective reduction of r-SDs for Fe3+ and Cu2+ (Scheme 1C). The analytical accuracy of the developed methods was evaluated by standard addition recovery experiments for environmental water samples. This work proposes a simple and novel strategy for the fabrication of optical probes based on reduced-state nanomaterials with reducibility and fluorescence properties for sensitive and selective analysis of metal ions.

The r-SDs were synthesized using modified methods from previous reports [9,12]. Briefly, NaOH (8.0 g) was dissolved into redistilled water (100.0 mL) with gentle magnetic stirring, followed by the addition of PEG-400 (6.0 mL) and sublimed sulfur powder (2.8 g) and reacted at 70 °C for 72 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was treated with a dialysis bag (Da. 3500) in redistilled water to remove impurities several times within 48 h. Finally, the brown solution was dried by a rotary evaporator and freeze-dried to obtain r-SDs with high fluorescence and promising reducibility.

The AuNPs/r-SDs can be easily and directly prepared in the presence of Au3+ and r-SDs on account of the promising reducibility of r-SDs. In brief, 250.0 μL of HAuCl4 (10.0 mmol/L) and 50.0 μL of r-SDs (10.0 mg/mL) were dissolved in 9.7 mL redistilled water and reacted at 60 °C for 1 h. After cooling to room temperature, the wine-red result solution was centrifuged at 6000 r/min for 12 min and redissolved into 10.0 mL to obtain the final AuNPs/r-SDs solution.

The sensors of T-AuNPs/r-SDs can be prepared based on the traditional amide reaction between –COOH (Thy-COOH) and –NH2 (Cy-AuNPs/r-SDs). The AuNPs/r-SDs were first modified by Cy through Au–S bonding. Then, 220 μL of Cy (100 μmol/L) was added to 10.0 mL of the above AuNPs/r-SDs solution with continuous stirring for 1 h to obtain Cy-modified AuNPs/r-SDs (Cy-AuNPs/r-SDs). At the same time, the –COOH groups belonging to Thy-COOH were activated by the mature EDC-NHS system. Briefly, 10.0 mg NHS and EDC were dissolved in 2.0 mL of MES solution (10.0 mmol/L, pH 6.0), followed by the addition of 2.0 mL Thy-COOH (0.2 mmol/L) for 30 min at room temperature. Finally, the T-AuNP/r-SD sensors can be formed by mixing the activated Thy-COOH (220.0 μL) with the above Cy-AuNP/r-SDs under continuous shaking for 4 h. After the centrifugation procedure, the precipitate was dissolved into 2.0 mL redistilled water to obtain the final T-AuNP/r-SD solution for Hg2+ analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The promising reducibility of r-SDs endows feasibility in the direct formation of AuNPs and reducing the valence of Fe3+ and Cu2+, exhibiting great potential in the sensitive and selective analysis of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+. The T-AuNPs/r-SDs can be used for the analysis of Hg2+ through special bonding between Hg2+ and thymine. The derivatization reaction occurs between reduced metal ions and derivatization reagents, including Fe2+-Phen and Cu+-Nc systems, resulting in the formation of new compounds as quenchers of r-SDs. Thus, the sensitive and selective analysis of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ can be realized based on the unique reducibility and satisfactory fluorescence properties of r-SDs. The detailed analysis process and practical applications are provided in the Supporting Information (SI).

In this study, the Gibbs free energy of the chemical reaction was calculated in detail by Gaussian 09 software based on density functional theory (DFT). The geometric structure optimization and binding energy were conducted by the B3LYP method with the 6–311 G+ (d, p) basis set. In particular, the LanL2DZ basis set was used for Hg.

The r-SDs were prepared with a typical reflux method (Scheme 1A). On the one hand, r-SDs exhibit typical and obvious fluorescence properties compared to traditional QDs. On the other hand, the coated reduced sulfur ions, including S2– and
S
O
3
2 -
, on the surface of r-SDs provide promising reducibility properties, indicating their great potential in the in situ reduction of Au3+ to AuNPs and reducing the valence states of metal ions, including Fe3+ and Cu2+. Thus, the sensitive and selective analysis of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ can be realized through T-AuNPs/r-SDs and the derivatization reaction of Fe2+-Phen and Cu+-Nc systems with r-SDs as fluorescent markers (Scheme 1B).

As shown in Fig. 1 A, the morphology and structure of r-SDs were measured by TEM and HRTEM. The r-SDs exhibit irregular shapes with diameters of approximately 2 nm, and a clear lattice structure with 0.209 nm can also be distinguished. Fig. 1B shows the optical properties of r-SDs, including the UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. A distinct absorption peak of r-SDs at approximately 265 nm was attributed to the n–π * transition of sulfur atoms. The r-SDs also exhibited obvious and traditional fluorescence characteristics with the best excitation wavelength of 360 nm and emission wavelength of 440 nm. A 3D contour map of r-SDs is shown in Fig. S1. The fluorescence intensity increases significantly as the excitation wavelength increases in the range of 250–360 nm and then slightly decreases above 360 nm. In addition, the calculated fluorescence quantum yield of r-SDs was approximately 7.4% using quinine sulfate as a reference (Fig. S2). The typical fluorescence properties of r-SDs ensure the feasibility of fluorescence detection combined with the promising reducibility of r-SDs.

Furthermore, the r-SDs were characterized by FT-IR (Fig. S3). The detailed characterization and corresponding description are supported in SI. In addition, the chemical composition and valence states of r-SDs were investigated by using XPS spectra. As displayed in Fig. 1 C, the r-SDs have three main composed elements, including O 1 s (554 eV), C 1 s (288 eV), and S 2p (167 eV). The detailed elemental contents are provided in Table S1. The binding energies of C 1 s at 286.31 eV and 284.75 eV are attributed to C–O and C–C, respectively (Fig. 1D). The high-resolution of the O 1 s peaks at 532.60 eV and 532.05 eV belong to C–O and
S
O
3
2 -
(Fig. 1E), respectively. The detailed XPS spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s further confirm the existence of PEG-400 on the surface of r-SDs, confirming the good dispersion and fluorescence properties of r-SDs. In addition, the high-resolution S 2p spectrum was composed of five main peaks at 168.87 eV, 167.73 eV, 163.50 eV, 164.70 eV, and 161.71 eV, attributed to
S
O
4
2 -
,
S
O
3
2 -
, atomic sulfur, and S2− or
S
x
2 -
, respectively (Fig. 1 F) [10,15,19]. The promising reducibility of r-SDs can be explained by various reduced sulfur ions (
S
O
3
2 -
, S2−, or
S
x
2 -
) on the surface of r-SDs.

The above results suggest the successful synthesis of r-SDs with typical fluorescence performance and promising reducibility, exhibiting great potential as fluorescent markers for the analysis of metal ions based on the applications of reducibility properties.

The photostability of r-SDs was evaluated under several conditions, including different kinds of inorganic salts, certain concentrations of NaCl, pH values, and exposure times. As shown in Fig. S4A and S4B, the tested inorganic salts and certain concentrations of NaCl showed negligible effects on the fluorescence intensity of r-SDs at 440 nm. In addition, different pH values in the range of 5–11 were adopted to simulate the complexity of the sample environment in real analysis applications. As displayed in Fig. S4C, the emission intensity of r-SDs still remained stable in the above pH ranges. Finally, the as-fabricated r-SDs were exposed to daylight lamps for different times to investigate the tolerability of photobleaching (Fig. S4D). The r-SDs exhibit excellent photostability after daylight lamp illumination within 24 h. The good photostability of r-SDs broadens the scope of analysis applications in complex samples.

The reduction property of r-SDs was evaluated and studied in detail. The r-SDs were first applied to the in situ reduction of Au3+ to AuNPs (AuNPs/r-SDs) based on the promising reduction property of r-SDs. Before the characterization of AuNPs/r-SDs, four main synthesis parameters, including the concentration of r-SDs, concentration of HAuCl4, incubation temperature, and incubation time, were optimized to obtain the best performance (Fig. S5). The as-fabricated AuNPs/r-SDs exhibits similar morphology of sphere shape (Fig. S6A and S6B) compared to other metal nanoparticles synthesized by QDs, [36,37] and the r-SDs were uniformly distributed on the surface of AuNPs (Fig. S6C). Compared to pure r-SDs, AuNPs/r-SDs show typical and obvious peaks of Au 4 f at 84.08 eV (Fig. S7A), and the detailed XPS spectra of Au 4 f at 87.48 eV and 83.80 eV can be attributed to Au 4 f5/2 and Au 4 f7/2, respectively (Fig. S7B). In addition, the AuNPs/r-SDs (−20.7 mV) also show lower zeta potentials than r-SDs (−18.2 mV), which is similar to traditional AuNPs synthesized by reducing agents, including sodium citrate, ascorbic acid, and sodium borohydride (Fig. S8). A similar phenomenon and characterization for the reduction of Ag+ to AgNPs can be seen in Fig. S9. The above results reveal that the as-developed r-SDs have excellent reducibility similar to that of typical reducing agents for the in situ reduction of noble metal ions to metal colloids.

The reduction mechanism of Au3+ to AuNPs by r-SDs was investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the pure HAuCl4 or r-SDs show negligible UV–vis characteristic peaks of AuNPs at approximately 530 nm. In addition, AuNPs can be synthesized by using alcohols as reducing agents, [38] suggesting the necessity for investigating PEG-400 distributed on the surface of r-SDs. Unfortunately, the coexistence of Au3+ and PEG-400 also shows negligible peaks of AuNPs. Thus, the r-SDs play a fundamental role in the reduction of Au3+ to AuNPs. Furthermore, Fig. 1 F shows the wide distribution of reduced sulfur ions (
S
O
3
2 -
, S2− or
S
x
2 -
) on the surface of r-SDs, indicating the great potential for reduction. As displayed in Fig. 2B, the pure S2− and S2− + PEG-400 exhibit negligible UV–vis characteristic peaks of AuNPs at approximately 530 nm with a similar color of light brown. In addition, the mixture of
S
O
3
2 -
+ Au3+ and
S
O
3
2 -
+ PEG-400 + Au3+ exhibits obvious peaks of AuNPs at approximately 530 nm with typical purple and dark purple colors, respectively. The higher and sharper absorption peak of group
S
O
3
2 -
+ PEG-400 compared to pure
S
O
3
2 -
suggests the better dispersity of AuNPs in the presence of PEG-400. The detailed XPS spectra of S 2p for AuNPs/r-SDs show that the content of
S
O
3
2 -
in AuNPs/r-SDs (Fig. 2 C) was lower than that in r-SDs (Fig. 1 F), indicating the formation of
S
O
4
2 -
oxidized from
S
O
3
2 -
. Hence, the reducibility of r-SDs for the in situ reduction of Au3+ to AuNPs mainly originated from
S
O
3
2 -
distributed on the surface of r-SDs.

In addition, the reduction property of r-SDs for reducing the valence states of metal ions, including Fe3+ and Cu2+, can also be found (Fig. 2D and G). In general, Phen can specifically bind Fe2+, producing a distinct UV–vis absorption peak at about 510 nm [39,40]. Similarly, the special binding can be found between Nc and Cu+, resulting obvious UV–vis absorption peak at about 460 nm [41,42]. As shown in Fig. 2D, the r-SDs, Phen, and Fe3+ have no UV–vis absorption peaks at approximately 510 nm. However, the mixture of Phen-Fe3+ solution exhibits a distinct absorption band at approximately 510 nm in the presence of r-SDs, suggesting the fundamental role of r-SDs in reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+. Moreover, a similar phenomenon can be found in the Nc-Cu2+-r-SDs system with UV–vis absorption peaks at approximately 460 nm (Fig. 2 G). Thus, the promising reducibility of r-SDs endows the ability to reduce the valence states of metal ions, including Fe3+ and Cu2+. In addition, two commonly used reducing agents, ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH), were applied to the reduction of Fe3+/Cu2+ to Fe2+/Cu+. As shown in Fig. S10, similar UV–vis absorption peaks at approximately 510 nm and 460 nm can be observed in the AA/GSH-Phen-Fe3+ and AA/GSH-Nc-Cu2+ systems, revealing the similar reducibility of r-SDs compared to commonly used reducing agents of AA and GSH. Finally, the feasibility of the reaction was further investigated by the electrode potential, as shown in Fig. S11. The corresponding electrode potentials are shown in Equations (1), (2), (3) and (4). In addition, the electrode potentials of the three reactions are both greater than 0 (Equations (5), (6) and (7)), indicating a spontaneous process of the three mentioned reactions and a consistent phenomenon, as shown in Fig. 2E and H.

Similar to AuNPs/r-SDs, the reducibility originating from reduced sulfur ions, including
S
O
3
2 -
or S2−, on the surface of r-SDs was investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 2E, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the Phen-Fe3+-S2– and Phen-Fe3+-
S
O
3
2 -
systems produce an obvious absorption peak at approximately 510 nm, revealing the effective reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. In addition, the intensity of the absorption peak Phen-Fe3+-S2– is higher than that of Phen-Fe3+-
S
O
3
2 -
, indicating the major role of S2– in the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p can be split into four peaks, corresponding to Fe2+ 2p3/2 (709.8 eV), Fe3+ 2p3/2 (712.6 eV), Fe2+ 2p1/2 (723.1 eV), and Fe3+ 2p1/2 (725.4 eV), further indicating the existence of Fe2+ after the addition of r-SDs into Phen-Fe3+ (Fig. 2 F) [43,44]. A similar investigation was also applied to the Nc-Cu2+ system (Fig. 2H). As shown in Fig. 2H, only the Nc-Cu2+-
S
O
3
2 -
system produced an obvious absorption peak at approximately 460 nm, suggesting the irreplaceable role of
S
O
3
2 -
in the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+. In addition, the detailed XPS spectra of Cu 2p can be divided into two peaks located at 533.0 eV and 952.6 eV, which are attributed to Cu+ 2p1/2 and Cu+ 2p3/2, respectively, confirming the existence of Cu+ after the addition of r-SDs into Nc-Cu2+ (Fig. 2I) [45,46]. The above results suggest that the as-fabricated r-SDs show excellent reducibility and great potential in the reduction of Au3+ to AuNPs and Fe3+/Cu2+ to Fe2+/Cu+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of an analytical method for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: In the following study, dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric probes were fabricated for the sensitive and selective analysis of Hg2+ based on the as-fabricated r-SDs and thymine-modified AuNPs/r-SDs (T-AuNPs/r-SDs). Hg2+ can be selectively captured by thymine, resulting in the aggregation of T-AuNPs/r-SDs. The aggregated T-AuNPs/r-SDs will decrease and/or redshift the UV–vis absorption peak of AuNPs as well as recover the fluorescence intensity of r-SDs quenched by AuNPs/r-SDs. Thus, the content of Hg2+ can be accurately quantified by reading the absorption intensity of AuNPs at approximately 530 nm and the fluorescence intensity of r-SDs at approximately 440 nm. The detailed thymine modification and Hg2+ recognition process were verified by DFT calculations ( Fig. 3).

In addition, the mechanism of Hg2+ sensing by dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric probes was further investigated. As displayed in Fig. S12A, the UV–vis spectra of T-AuNPs/r-SDs show significant overlap of the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of r-SDs. The fluorescence lifetimes of r-SDs, r-SDs + T-AuNPs/r-SDs, and r-SDs + T-AuNPs/r-SDs + Hg2+ were 2.21 ns, 2.05 ns, and 2.17 ns, respectively (Fig. S12B), which can be combined with overlapping spectra (Fig. S12A) to explain the sensing mechanism of Hg2+ with the inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, static quenching effect (SQE) can be proposed by combining the phenomenon of changed UV–vis spectra after the addition of Hg2+ (Fig. S13B) with unchanged fluorescence lifetimes. Furthermore, the aggregation status of T-AuNPs/r-SDs was measured by DLS spectroscopy before and after the addition of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. S12C, the hydrodynamic diameter of T-AuNPs/r-SDs increased from 69.4 nm to 90.12 nm as the concentration of Hg2+ increased from the blank to 200 nmol/L, indicating the aggregation of T-AuNPs/r-SDs and a decrease in the UV–vis absorption peak. The feasibility of the developed dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric probes was further applied to sensing Hg2+ by fluorescence and colorimetric analysis (Fig. S13).

Next, the developed dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric probes were used to establish the fitting linearity curve between fluorescence or absorption intensity and the concentration of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of r-SDs at approximately 440 nm increased gradually (slightly redshifted) with increasing concentrations of Hg2+. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectra of T-AuNPs/r-SDs at approximately 530 nm gradually decreased along with an increase at approximately 700 nm with increasing Hg2+ concentration (Fig. 4B). A satisfactory linear relationship between the F/F
0 value and the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 5.0–70.0 nmol/L with a linear fitting curve of F/F
0 = 0.0068c + 0.9655 (R = 0.9978) could be obtained (Fig. 4 C). Similarly, the calibration plot equation of (A
530
/A
700 )/(A
530
/A
700 )0 = −0.0010c + 1.0109 (R = 0.9978) with a detection range of 10.0–500.0 nmol/L for colorimetric probes can be found (Fig. 4D). The limit of detection (LOD) for fluorescent and colorimetric probes was calculated to be 0.82 nmol/L and 4.05 nmol/L, respectively, by the rule of 3Sb/k, which is lower than the maximum allowable concentrations of Hg2+ in drinking water permitted by the WHO and EPA. Furthermore, the developed fluorescence and colorimetric method for Hg2+ sensing shows advantages of a wider linear range and higher sensitivity compared to previously reported methods (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of an analytical method for Fe3+ and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the excellent reducibility and satisfactory fluorescence property of r-SDs, which have been proven clearly above, two fluorescent systems, r-SDs-Phen-Fe3+ and r-SDs-Nc-Cu2+, were developed for the quantitative analysis of Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. In general, Fe3+ and Cu2+ can be reduced to Fe2+ and Cu+, followed by a derivatization reaction with Phen and Nc to generate Phen-Fe2+ and Nc-Cu+, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra of Phen-Fe2+ and Nc-Cu+ show significant spectra overlapping with the excitation and emission wavelengths of r-SDs, resulting in obvious fluorescence quenching induced by IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Fig. S14A). In addition, the fluorescence lifetimes of r-SDs, r-SDs + Phen + Fe3+ and r-SDs + Nc + Cu2+ further confirmed the quenching mechanism of IFE (Fig. S14B). Moreover, SQE also can be proposed by combining the changed UV–vis spectra of r-SDs-Phen-Fe3+ (Fig. 2D) and r-SDs-Nc-Cu2+ (Fig. 2 G) system with unchanged fluorescence lifetimes. Thus, the concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ can be accurately determined by recording the fluorescence intensity of r-SDs at approximately 440 nm. The feasibility of the developed fluorescent probes, including r-SDs-Phen and r-SDs-Nc, was further applied to the analysis of Fe3+ and Cu2+ (Fig. S15).

Before the quantitative detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+, analytical optimization conditions, including pH values and incubation time, were optimized to achieve the best sensitivity (Fig. S16, S17). In the following, the developed r-SDs-Phen and r-SDs-Nc probes were applied to investigate the linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity and concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 5A and B, the fluorescence intensity of r-SDs at approximately 440 nm decreased with increasing concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+. In addition, the linear relationship between the fluorescence ratio (F
0
/F) and the concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ with fitting curves of F
0
/F = 0.0146c + 1.0260 (R = 0.9967) and F
0
/F = 0.0188c + 1.0117 (R = 0.9982) in the range of 1.0–100.0 μmol/L and 1.0–50.0 μmol/L were established (Fig. 5C, D). The LODs for Fe3+ and Cu2+ were 0.36 μmol/L and 0.56 μmol/L after calculation by rules of 3Sb/k, respectively. Compared with previous reports, our results show better analytical performance in the linear range and sensitivity for the analysis of Fe3+ and Cu2+ (Tables S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the developed probes
ce:section.ce:para: In real environmental samples, the complexity of interfering substances will limit the analytical performance. Thus, the selectivity of dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric probes of T-AuNPs/r-SDs for Hg2+ were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6A, the as-developed T-AuNPs/r-SDs exhibit high selectivity for Hg2+ compared to interfering metal ions with a high concentration of 100-fold. In addition, the selectivity of the developed r-SDs-Phen and r-SDs-Nc probes for Fe3+ and Cu2+ was also investigated. As displayed in Fig. 6B and Fig. 6C, the r-SDs-Phen and r-SDs-Nc probes exhibit high specificity for Fe3+ and Cu2+ compared to other metal ions. Finally, all of the developed optical probes, including T-AuNPs/r-SDs, r-SDs-Phen and r-SDs-Nc, show promising salinity tolerance with concentrations of NaCl as high as 10 mmol/L (Fig. 6A–C). The above results prove the good selectivity and stability of the as-developed probes, indicating their feasibility in the practical analysis of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications in environmental water sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, the as-developed T-AuNPs/r-SDs, r-SDs-Phen and r-SDs-Nc were applied to detect Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ in environmental water samples to confirm their practicability. As shown in Table S3, the recoveries for Hg2+ by dual-mode fluorescence and colorimetric probes of T-AuNPs/r-SDs were in the range of 94.9–108.8% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) ranging from 1.1% to 6.8%. Apart from Hg2+, two other metal ions, Fe3+ and Cu2+, were also investigated by similar standard addition recovery experiments (Table S4). The recoveries for Fe3+ and Cu2+ ranged from 96.0% to 108.6%, and the RSDs were in the range of 0.1–2.2%. The above results further confirm the feasibility of the as-developed optical probes in the practical analysis of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ in real samples.

In conclusion, reduced-state sulfur nanodots (r-SDs) with promising reducibility, satisfactory fluorescence properties and high stability were prepared for the design and fabrication of optical probes for the sensitive and selective analysis of metal ions, including Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+. The detailed reduction mechanisms and fluorescence performance of r-SDs were investigated by several characterizations. The reducibility of r-SDs can reduce Au3+ to AuNPs (AuNPs/r-SDs) in situ and reduce Fe3+ and Cu2+ to Fe2+ and Cu+, respectively. Based on the excellent reducibility and fluorescence property of r-SDs, dual-mode fluorescence-colorimetric probes and fluorescent probes were designed and fabricated for quantitative analysis of Hg2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ with high performance. The standard addition recovery experiments for environmental water samples confirm the accuracy of the developed method. The satisfactory analytical performance of the as-fabricated probes originated from the integration of the promising reducibility and satisfactory fluorescence property of r-SDs. This work proposes a novel strategy for the design and fabrication of optical probes based on nanomaterials with reducibility and fluorescence properties for the analysis of metal ions with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Hanqiang Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Yufei Li: Data curation, Formal analysis. Feng Gan: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University for supporting this research. The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for materials characterizations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132817.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

Sreeprased, Facile synthesis of copper doped carbon dots and their application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe in the detection of Fe3+ ions, RSC Adv., 6 (2016) 28745–28750, https://doi.org/10.1039/C5RA27658F.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cu-doped carbon dots as catalysts for the chemiluminescence detection of glucose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Duan, Y. Huang, S. Chen, W. Zuo, B. Shi, Cu-Doped Carbon Dots as Catalysts for the Chemiluminescence Detection of Glucose, ACS Omega, 4 (2019) 9911–9917, https://doi.org/10.1021/acsomega.9b00738.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hanqiang Zhang received his Master’s Degree in 2015 and then worked in College of Chemistry and Materials, Longyan University. At present, he is studying for his Doctor's Degree in Sun Yat-Sen University. His research interests include material synthesis, electrochemistry sensor, fluorescence and colorimetric sensor.

Yufei Li received her Bachelor’s Degree in 2020. At present, she is studying for her Master’s Degree in Sun Yat-Sen University. Her research interests include chemometric, fluorescent nanomaterials for analytical and applications.

Feng Gan is a professor in School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-Sen University. He received his Doctor’s Degree in 2001. His research interests include chemometrics and fabrication of fluorescent nanomaterial for analytical and bioanalytical applications.
